perturbation.original_text: H.J.G. shall keep the Confidential Information confidential and shall not disclose such Confidential Information, in whole or in part, to any person other than its Representatives who need to know such Confidential Information in connection with H.I.G.’ S evaluation, negotiation and potential consummation of the Transaction, except with the prior written consent of COMVERGE or as otherwise permitted hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: H.J.G. shall endeavor to keep the Confidential Information confidential and shall try not to disclose such Confidential Information, in whole or in part, to any person other than its Representatives who need to know such Confidential Information in connection with H.I.G.’ S evaluation, negotiation and potential consummation of the Transaction, except with the prior written consent of COMVERGE or as otherwise permitted hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall keep' to 'shall endeavor to keep' and 'shall not disclose' to 'shall try not to disclose' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. This makes the obligation discretionary rather than mandatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. By weakening the non-disclosure obligation to 'endeavor' and 'try,' the modified text potentially fails to meet the 'reasonable measures' standard required under the DTSA for protecting confidential information that could be considered a trade secret.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. By changing 'shall keep' to 'shall endeavor to keep' and 'shall not disclose' to 'shall try not to disclose', the altered text introduces ambiguity, making the confidentiality obligation discretionary rather than mandatory. The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, and the modified text potentially fails to meet this 'reasonable measures' standard. The scraped snippets do not directly address the DTSA requirements but the law explanation does, outlining the change's likely failure to reasonably protect trade secrets, and the perturbation explanation confirms the weakening of the obligation. This creates a legal contradiction by potentially undermining the required protection of confidential information under the DTSA.

perturbation.original_text: No later than ten (10) business days following the receipt of a written request from COMVERGE, H.I.G. shall destroy or deliver to COMVERGE all Confidential Information, together with a certificate executed by the agent and/or representative or principal of H.I.G. confirming that all such materials in H.I.G.’s possession or control have been delivered to COMVERGE or destroyed, except as permitted herein.
perturbation.changed_text: H.I.G. will make an attempt, as needed, to destroy or deliver to COMVERGE all Confidential Information within a reasonable timeframe after a request from COMVERGE, alongside a document that loosely confirms what happened to those files. This is, of course, unless the files fall under one of the agreed-upon exceptions.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall destroy or deliver' to 'make an attempt...to destroy or deliver', 'ten (10) business days' to 'reasonable timeframe', 'a certificate executed by the agent' to 'a document that loosely confirms' introduces significant ambiguity regarding the obligation to return or destroy confidential information. This weakens the legal obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Georgia Uniform Trade Secrets Act (GUTSA) aims to protect trade secrets. Delaying the process of returning the files, delivering a document that doesn't ensure an accurate reflection of events, and stating that returning/destroying confidential info is only done "as needed" all work against the purpose of maintaining confidential information, thus failing the obligation to protect trade secrets under GUTSA. The original agreement is much stricter, which is why the modified text would contradict the state law.

Definitions Universal Citation: GA Code § 10-1-761 (2020) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next As used in this article, the term: "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to maintain secrecy or limit use, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering of a trade secret not acquired by misappropriation or independent development shall not be considered improper means. "Misappropriation" means: Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: Used improper means to acquire knowledge of a trade secret; At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that knowledge of the trade secret was: Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or Before a material change of position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, as
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity that contradicts the intent of protecting trade secrets, as explained in the Law Explanation. The original text had a definitive timeline ('ten (10) business days') and required a certificate confirming destruction or delivery. The changed text uses vague language like 'reasonable timeframe' and 'a document that loosely confirms', which weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. Snippet 2 defines 'Improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to maintain secrecy or limit use'. The changes weaken the duty to maintain secrecy, which could lead to a breach. Therefore, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: H.I.G. will not export, directly or indirectly, any Confidential information acquired from COMVERGE pursuant to this Agreement, or any product utilizing such Confidential information, to any country, or any company located in any country, for which the U.S. Government or any agency thereof at the time of export requires an export license or other governmental approval without first obtaining written consent from COMVERGE and the appropriate license.
perturbation.changed_text: H.I.G. will generally refrain from exporting Confidential information acquired from COMVERGE pursuant to this Agreement, or any product utilizing such Confidential information, to countries where the U.S. Government *probably* requires an export license. This is, however, only to the extent that it is feasible to obtain written consent from COMVERGE beforehand.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will not export' to 'will generally refrain from exporting' makes compliance discretionary. Adding 'probably' makes the standard unclear. Adding 'only to the extent that it is feasible' provides an easy escape clause. This weakens the export restriction.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Export Administration Regulations (EAR) control the export of certain items, including technical data, for national security and foreign policy reasons. Making export restrictions discretionary contradicts the mandatory nature of the EAR, which requires strict adherence to licensing requirements. The modified text's vague language undermines the EAR's enforcement.

perturbation.justification: The original text contains a definitive restriction on exporting confidential information without consent and proper licensing. The changed text weakens this restriction by introducing discretionary language ('generally refrain'), subjective assessment ('probably requires'), and an escape clause ('only to the extent that it is feasible'). According to the Law Explanation, the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) require strict adherence to licensing requirements, and discretionary language undermines this enforcement. Although the snippets do not explicitly define 'strict adherence', snippet 2 indicates that the EAR, under 15 CFR 730-774, guides export regulations, and the changes significantly weaken compliance with these regulations, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: For the purpose of complying with the obligations set forth herein, H.I.G. shall use a reasonable standard of care, no less than efforts commensurate with those that it employs for the protection of its own confidential and sensitive information.
perturbation.changed_text: For the purpose of complying with the obligations set forth herein, H.I.G. will consider using what it thinks is a reasonable standard of care, which can be similar to the efforts used for its own information, whatever those may be.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall use' to 'will consider using' makes the standard of care discretionary. Replacing 'no less than efforts commensurate with those that it employs' with 'can be similar to' weakens the requirement. Adding 'whatever those may be' further reduces the clarity and enforceability of the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Georgia's Trade Secrets Act requires the implementation of reasonable measures. 'Will consider using' and 'can be similar to' does not constitute 'reasonable measures' to protect trade secrets, particularly when the actual security measures used by H.I.G. are left undefined ('whatever those may be'). This ambiguity undermines the necessary level of protection required by the statute.

Reverse engineering of a trade secret not acquired by misappropriation or independent development shall not be considered improper means. "Misappropriation" means: Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: Used improper means to acquire knowledge of a trade secret; At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that knowledge of the trade secret was: Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or Before a material change of position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, as
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall use' to 'will consider using' and the weakening of the standard of care to 'can be similar to' introduces an ambiguity that contradicts the requirement for reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The addition of 'whatever those may be' further undermines the enforceability of the obligation, as the specific security measures are left undefined. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'Misappropriation' which relates to the acquisition and disclosure of trade secrets, implying a need for defined measures to prevent such actions. The modified contract language introduces a significant ambiguity that weakens the protection of trade secrets.
